items.q: If a complex ion has a coordination number of 4, what shape does it have?
items.correct: It can be either tetrahedral OR square planar — the shape depends on the metal and ligands (e.g. [Pt(NH3)4]2+ is square planar; [CuCl4]2- is tetrahedral).
items.misconception: A coordination number of 4 always means tetrahedral, because that gives the maximum bond-angle separation.
items.random_wrong: A coordination number of 4 always means square planar because complexes prefer to be flat.
items.why: Students apply VSEPR-style reasoning and default to tetrahedral, missing that d8 metals like Pt(II) form square planar complexes for electronic reasons.

items.q: Why are most transition metal complex ions coloured?
items.correct: Ligands split the d-orbitals into two energy levels; electrons absorb visible light to jump between them (d-d transitions), and the complementary colour is transmitted.
items.misconception: Transition metal ions are coloured because they have a half-full d-subshell which naturally absorbs light.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals are coloured because their nuclei emit photons in the visible range.
items.why: Students see 'd-block = coloured' as a label but miss that colour requires a partially filled d-subshell AND ligand-induced splitting — Sc3+ and Zn2+ are colourless because the d level is empty or full.

items.q: Why can transition metals show variable oxidation states?
items.correct: The 4s and 3d orbitals are similar in energy, so different numbers of electrons can be removed without a large energy cost.
items.misconception: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they have many shells of electrons.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they can gain protons easily.
items.why: Students attribute it vaguely to 'lots of electrons' rather than the specific 4s/3d energy proximity that makes successive ionisations comparable in cost.
Correct answer: cis isomer. “trans isomer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Optical. “E/Z” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
